title: Research
text:
Throughout the summer of 2025, I conducted research on statistical algorithms in the context of sports ranking. After acquiring and tidying a dataset on all men’s international soccer matches between 2018-2022, I isolated features of each match that I deemed to be core predictors for the results of the 2024 World Cup seedings (Date, Winner, Goal differential, Home/Away/Neutral, and Match type). The ratings/rankings generated by numerous algorithms were all compared to the actual 2024 World Cup seedings and the actual results of the 2024 World Cup. It turned out the Cauchy Model
Here is a teaser of my implementation of my first chosen model, least squares regression (Notice how its generated rankings aren’t very believable yet):

library(tidyverse)

library(MASS)


Attaching package: 'MASS'

The following object is masked from 'package:dplyr':

    select

# read in games data
Games_Data <- read.csv("New International Games 2018-2022.csv")

## Function to read scores from Games_Data data frame with team names and margin and calculate rankings

Rankings <- function(Games_Data){
  
  #Preliminary work to set up X matrix
  Scores <- Games_Data |> dplyr::select(home_team, away_team, home, margin)
  names(Scores) <- c("TEAM", "OPP",  "Mar", "Home")
  Scores <- rbind(Scores,Scores)
  h <- nrow(Scores)/2
  Scores$TEAM <- as.character(Scores$TEAM)
  Scores$OPP <- as.character(Scores$OPP)
  Scores$TEAM[(h+1):nrow(Scores)] <- Scores$OPP[1:h]
  Scores$OPP[(h+1):nrow(Scores)] <- Scores$TEAM[1:h]
  Scores$TEAM <- as.factor(Scores$TEAM)
  Scores$OPP <- as.factor(Scores$OPP)
  Scores <- Scores[!is.na(Scores$Mar),]
  games <- nrow(Scores)/2
  teams <- c(Scores$TEAM)
  opps <- c(Scores$OPP)
  numteams <- length(unique(teams))
  X <- matrix(0, games+1, numteams+1)
  
  # fill in X matrix with 0's and 1's
  for (i in 1:games){
    team <- teams[i]
    opp <- opps[i]
    X[i,team] <- 1
    X[i,opp] <- -1
  }
  X[games+1,1:numteams]=c(rep(1, numteams)) #add sum to zero constraint
  X[,numteams+1]=c(Scores$Home[1:games],0) #add homefield advantage
  y <- c(Scores$Mar[1:games],0) # margin of victory vector
  
  b <- ginv(t(X)%*%X)%*%t(X)%*%y  #calculate ratings using least squares
  
  # organize into table
  Rankings_Table <- data.frame(sort(as.character(unique(Scores$TEAM))), b[1:numteams])
  names(Rankings_Table) <- c("Team", "Rating")
  Rankings_Table <- Rankings_Table[order(-Rankings_Table$Rating), ]
  Rankings_Table$Rnk <- (seq(1,nrow(Rankings_Table), 1))
  return(Rankings_Table)
}


# calculate rankings
World22_Rankings <- Rankings(Games_Data)
World22_Rankings

Here is the final poster I completed alongside Alibek Mamyrbay to summarize our work.

title: Owen Horton
text: Welcome! I’m Owen Horton, an aspiring data consultant pursuing a degree in Mathematics and Data Science with a minor in Geoscience at Lawrence University. I am a rising senior looking to secure a position in a company that will bring meaning to my career. Whether I utilize machine learning methods for marketing strategies within a large business, probabilistic analysis in customer assistance for a financial institution, or data-scientific journals to promote environmental conservation, I’m excited to contribute my skills with a team and help change the world. My professional links and sources are below, but there is much more I have to share! I would be honored if you checked out my other pages of past projects, research, and hobbies!
